coredata.dc:title: A facile approach for sulphur and nitrogen co-doped carbon nanodots to improve photothermal eradication of drug-resistant bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xinyue
Liu, Huaze
Wang, Yu
Zheng, Xueliang
Xu, Hui
Ding, Juan
Sun, Jie
Jiang, Tingting
Li, Qin
Liu, Yang
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we produced S, N co-doped CNDs (SN@CNDs) by using dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and formamide (FA) as single sources of S and N, respectively. We varied the S/N ratios by adjusting the volume ratios of DMSO and FA and investigated their effect on the red-shift of the CNDs' absorption peak. Our findings demonstrate that SN@CNDs synthesized using a volume ratio of 5:6 between DMSO and FA exhibit the most significant absorption peak redshift and enhanced near-infrared absorption performance. Based on comparative analysis of the particle size, surface charge, and fluorescence spectrum of the S@CNDs, N@CNDs, and SN@CNDs, we propose a possible mechanism to explain the change of optical properties of CNDs due to S, N doping. Co-doping creates a more uniform and smaller band gap, resulting in a shift of the Fermi level and a change in energy dissipation from radioactive to non-radiative decay. Importantly, the as-prepared SN@CNDs exhibited a photothermal conversion efficiency of 51.36% at 808 nm and demonstrated exceptional photokilling effects against drug-resistant bacteria in both in vitro and in vivo experiments. Our facile method for synthesizing S and N co-doped CNDs can be extended to the preparation of other S and N co-doped nanomaterials, potentially improving their performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Sulphur and nitrogen co-doping
Photothermal therapy
Drug-resistant bacteria

Experiments and methods
Synthesis of S, N co-doped CNDs
Characterization

Bacterial culture
In vitro photothermal therapy of S, N co-doped CNDs against VRE bacteria

Biosafety and biocompatibility of S, N co-doped CNDs
Cytotoxicity and phototoxicity of S, N co-doped CNDs
In vitro hemolysis of S, N co-doped CNDs
In vivo biosafety of S, N co-doped CNDs
In vivo antibacterial photothermal therapy of S, N co-doped CNDs
Results and discussion
Synthesis and characterizations of S, N co-doped CNDs
Photothermal performance of S, N co-doped CNDs
In vitro photothermal therapy of S, N co-doped CNDs against VRE bacteria
Biosafety and biocompatibility of S, N co-doped CNDs
In vivo antibacterial photothermal therapy of S, N co-doped CNDs
Conclusions
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgments
Supplementary data
References

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We appreciate the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China ( ZR2020MC079 , ZR2014BP004 , ZR2020MB075 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21505066 ), a project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program, China ( J18KA265 ) and a project of Shandong Innovation and Entrepreneurship Community of Antibody Drug .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis (a) and fluorescent (b) spectra of all CNDs. The concentration of all samples is 160 μg/mL.
TEM images (a), size distribution (b), zeta-potential (c), EDS (d), and FTIR spectra of S@CNDs, N@CNDs, and SN@CNDs.

Photothermal performance of SN@CNDs including photothermal images (a), a 3D plot of temperature variation with time and concentration (b), the temperature variation of various samples (c), and calculation of the photothermal conversion efficiency. Red line: temperature elevations of SN@CNDs when the laser was turned on and switched off. Blue line: The linear relationship between cooling time and the negative natural logarithm of driving force temperature (−ln(θ)), in which time constant (τs) could be determined to evaluate the heat transfer of the SN@CNDs. The NIR irradiation was performed by an 808 nm laser with 0.8 W/cm2 for 10 min. The concentration of samples is 160 μg/mL. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

In vitro antimicrobial photokilling effect of SN@CNDs including live/dead fluorescent images (a)–(h), photos of bacterial culture plates (i)–(p), bacterial viability (q), and growth curves (r). The NIR irradiation was performed by an 808 nm laser with 0.8 W/cm2 for 10 min. The concentration of samples is 160 μg/mL.

Flow cytometry analysis of VRE bacteria with various treatments including PBS, S@CNDs, N@CNDs, and SN@CNDs upon NIR irradiation or in the dark. The NIR irradiation was performed by an 808 nm laser with 0.8 W/cm2 for 10 min. The concentration of samples is 160 μg/mL.

In vivo antimicrobial photothermal treatment of SN@CNDs including photothermal images (left), photographs, schematics diagrams (middle), and H&E staining (right) of infected tissues treated with or without NIR irradiation. PBS served as a control group. The NIR irradiation was performed by an 808 nm laser with 0.8 W/cm2 for 10 min. The concentration of samples is 160 μg/mL.

head.ce:title: A facile approach for sulphur and nitrogen co-doped carbon nanodots to improve photothermal eradication of drug-resistant bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Ludong University, Yantai, 264025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Green Energy Materials and Battery Cascade Utilization, School of Intelligent Manufacturing, Huzhou College, Huzhou, 313000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Green Energy Materials and Battery Cascade Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Engineering Technology, Huzhou College, Huzhou 313000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xinyue Liu and Huaze Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we produced S, N co-doped CNDs (SN@CNDs) by using dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and formamide (FA) as single sources of S and N, respectively. We varied the S/N ratios by adjusting the volume ratios of DMSO and FA and investigated their effect on the red-shift of the CNDs' absorption peak. Our findings demonstrate that SN@CNDs synthesized using a volume ratio of 5:6 between DMSO and FA exhibit the most significant absorption peak redshift and enhanced near-infrared absorption performance. Based on comparative analysis of the particle size, surface charge, and fluorescence spectrum of the S@CNDs, N@CNDs, and SN@CNDs, we propose a possible mechanism to explain the change of optical properties of CNDs due to S, N doping. Co-doping creates a more uniform and smaller band gap, resulting in a shift of the Fermi level and a change in energy dissipation from radioactive to non-radiative decay. Importantly, the as-prepared SN@CNDs exhibited a photothermal conversion efficiency of 51.36% at 808 nm and demonstrated exceptional photokilling effects against drug-resistant bacteria in both in vitro and in vivo experiments. Our facile method for synthesizing S and N co-doped CNDs can be extended to the preparation of other S and N co-doped nanomaterials, potentially improving their performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Photothermal therapy (PTT) is a non-invasive therapeutic technique that utilizes photothermal therapy agents (PTAs) to convert light into heat within a short duration of minutes [1–4]. The heat generated by PTAs has the capability to irreversibly denature cellular proteins and rupture cell membranes, making it an efficacious treatment for drug-resistant bacterial and tumor cells [5–7]. Consequently, PTT is extensively used to treat resistance in bacterial and tumor cells [8,9]. Thus far, PTAs can be broadly categorized into inorganic nanomaterials (e.g., gold nanoparticles, copper sulfide nanoparticles) [10–12] and organic nanocomposites (e.g., polyaniline, porphyrin) [13,14]. However, their low solubility, high toxicity, and poor stability have hindered their application in vivo.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are an emerging carbon-based nanomaterial that has been widely used in bio-imaging, ion detection, and drug delivery owing to their good solubility, excellent photostability, low cost, and good biocompatibility [15,16]. However, CNDs' absorption peaks are primarily located in the ultraviolet (UV) to the green light range, leading to significant absorption and scattering in biological tissues, which restrict their potential in vivo therapeutic applications [15,17]. Thus, there is a pressing need to improve the near-infrared (NIR) absorption of CNDs.

In recent years, elemental doping has emerged as an effective strategy to improve the optical, electrical, and chemical properties of CNDs [18,19]. Common doping agents include nitrogen (N), sulphur (S), phosphorus (P), and others [19–21]. Doped CNDs can be classified as single- or multi-doped based on the number of introduced heteroatoms [16]. Several studies have reported on the photothermal conversion mechanism of doped CNDs [22,23]. Multi-doped CNDs have unique electronic structures and surface defects resulting from the synergistic effect of various heteroatoms, leading to altered energy dissipation modes and improved near-infrared absorption and photothermal conversion properties [16,24]. S, N co-doped CNDs (SN@CNDs) have been used for biomedical applications, as S and N elements are key components of proteins and nucleic acids in living organisms [25–29]. The metabolic pathways of proteins and nucleic acids in the body can accommodate doped S and N elements, leading to low bio-toxicity and good biocompatibility [30,31]. Several methods have been used to produce SN@CNDs. For instance, Zhao et al. utilized a one-step synthetic process involving lysine, o-phenylenediamine, and sulphuric acid to synthesize SN@CNDs [32], while Sun et al. employed citric acid and thiourea [33]. These methods lack a single source of sulphur or nitrogen, which makes it impossible to produce SN@CNDs with varying S, N ratios. This limitation impedes effective comparative analysis to assess the effect of S, N doping on CNDs. Therefore, it is crucial to establish an experimental methodology that can be systematically adopted to investigate the impact of S, and N ratios on the absorption properties and photothermal conversion efficiency of doped CNDs.

In this study, we produced doped CNDs by using dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and formamide (FA) as single sources of S and N, respectively. We varied the S/N ratios by adjusting the volume ratios of DMSO and FA and investigated their effect on the red-shift of the CNDs' absorption peak. Our findings demonstrate that SN@CNDs synthesized using a volume ratio of 5:6 between DMSO and FA exhibit the most significant absorption peak redshift and enhanced near-infrared absorption performance. Through comparative analysis of the particle size, surface charge, and fluorescence spectrum of the S@CNDs, N@CNDs, and SN@CNDs, we propose a possible mechanism to explain the change of optical properties of CNDs due to S, N doping. Co-doping creates a more uniform and smaller band gap, resulting in a shift of the Fermi level and a change in energy dissipation from radioactive to non-radiative decay. Importantly, the as-prepared SN@CNDs exhibited a photothermal conversion efficiency of 51.36% at 808 nm and demonstrated exceptional photokilling effects against drug-resistant bacteria in both in vitro and in vivo experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiments and methods
Synthesis of S, N co-doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The anhydrous citric acid (0.5 g) was dissolved in different volume ratios of formamide (FA) and DMSO mixture (11 mL). The volume ratios of DMSO and FA were 0:11, 2:9, 5:6, 9:2, and 11:0, respectively. After that, the solutions were heated at 160 °C for 4 h in a Teflon autoclave. The resulting product was dialyzed in double distilled water for 24 h (1000 Da cut-off molecular weight) and then lyophilized to yield brown powder as S, N co-doped CNDs. According to the volume ratio of DMSO and FA used in the reactions, they are labeled CNDs (11:0), CNDs (2:9), CNDs (5:6), CNDs (9:2), and CNDs (11:0), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous solutions of five kinds of CNDs (160 μg/mL) were used to collect UV–Vis spectra (TU-1810PC spectrometer, Purkinje, China). Three CNDs were used to collect fluorescence spectra at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm (Cary Eclipse spectrometer, Agilent, Japan). Moreover, transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200x, FEI, Holland) was used to analyze the particle size and morphology of three CNDs. The energy dispersive spectra (EDS) of the three CNDs were measured using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU8010, Hitachi, Japan). The zeta potentials of three CNDs were determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS, Malvern ZetaSizer ZS2000, UK). Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectra of the three CNDs were collected using a spectrometer (Magna 550, Nicolay, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance of S, N co-doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Three CNDs solutions (160 μg/mL) were irradiated by a NIR laser (808 nm, 0.8 W/cm2) and the temperature changes of the solutions were recorded using a thermocouple microprobe (Thermocouple microprobes (UT325, UNI-T, China)) and thermal imaging camera (Compact pro, Seek, USA). We also measured the temperature changes of the solutions with different concentrations of CNDs(5:6) including 0 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL, 160 μg/mL, and 320 μg/mL. An aqueous solution of CNDs(5:6) (160 μg/mL) was irradiated under an 808 nm NIR laser with different laser power including 0.6 W/cm2, 0.8 W/cm2, 1.0 W/cm2, and 1.2 W/cm2. Temperature changes of CNDs(5:6) (160 μg/mL) were also recorded under repeated on/off 808 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W/cm2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial culture
ce:section.ce:para: The vancomycin-resistant enterococci (VRE, ATCC51299) were cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium for 12 h at 37 °C. The bacterial solution was washed with PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4) and re-suspended and adjusted to OD600 of 0.5, in which the concentration of the bacterial solution was 2 × 107 CFU/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal therapy of S, N co-doped CNDs against VRE bacteria
Fluorescent staining and flow cytometry of live/dead bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: A bac-light bacterial viability kit was used for fluorescent staining of live/dead bacteria, in which SYTO-9 and propidium iodide (PI) are used to stain live and dead bacteria, respectively. 500 μL of bacterial solution (1 × 107 CFU/mL, 0.85% NaCl) was incubated with three CNDs (160 μg/mL) in the dark or irradiated with NIR light (808 nm, 0.8 W/cm2) for 10 min. After washing with NaCl solution (0.85%), 1.5 μL of a dye mixture with SYTO-9 (0.9 μM) and PI (10 μM) was added and incubated for 15 min in the dark. Bacterial fluorescent images and viability were analyzed using fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard plate colony counting and bacterial growth curves
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial viability was examined by standard plate counting. 500 μL of bacterial solution (1 × 107 CFU/mL, 10 mM PBS buffer) was incubated with three CNDs (160 μg/mL) in the dark or under NIR irradiation (808 nm, 0.8 W/cm2) for 10 min. Afterward, the bacterial solutions were diluted 100-fold with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) and the samples (50 μL) were separately spread on solid LB agar plates. After overnight culture at 37 °C, the colony-forming units (CFUs) formed on the plates were counted to assess the viability of the bacteria.

In addition, after photothermal treatment of the three CNDs, 50 μL of the diluted bacterial solution was added to 10 mL of LB liquid medium and incubated at 37 °C with shaking at 200 rpm. We recorded the OD600 value of the bacterial solutions every 1 h to plot the bacterial growth curves. All data were analyzed and processed using GraphPad Prism 8 software and significance analysis was performed using SPSS17.0 (∗p < 0.5, ∗∗p < 0.01, and ∗∗∗p < 0.001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosafety and biocompatibility of S, N co-doped CNDs
Cytotoxicity and phototoxicity of S, N co-doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity and phototoxicity of three CNDs were evaluated based on 3–4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays [34]. The mouse embryonic fibroblasts 3T3 cells (5 × 104 cells/well) were cultured in 96-well plates overnight at 37 °C in a 5% CO2. After the addition of CNDs (5:6) (200 μL, 0–160 μg/mL in PBS), the cells were incubated in the dark for 30 min and treated with or without NIR irradiation (808 nm, 0.8 W/cm2) for 10 min. After washing with PBS buffer, the cells were incubated with MTT solution (0.8 mg/mL) for 4 h. Next, DMSO was added and the absorbance at 490 nm was collected to evaluate cell viability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro hemolysis of S, N co-doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Murine red blood cells (RBCs) were used to evaluate in vitro hemolysis of three CNDs according to previous reports [35,36]. RBCs (900 μL) were washed with PBS buffer and incubated with various aqueous solutions (100 μL) including distilled water (H2O), PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4), CNDs (0:11), CNDs(5:6) and CNDs(11:0) for 1 h at 37 °C. Next, after washing with PBS buffer, the RBC solutions were diluted 5-fold to collect the absorbance at 492 nm. The hemolysis percentage was calculated according to the following formula: hemolysis percentage (%) = (Asample – APBS) ÷ (AH2O– APBS) × 100%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biosafety of S, N co-doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the in vivo biosafety of CNDs, we performed hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining tests [37]. After anesthetized with isoflurane, the hind limbs of BALB/C mice (females, 16–22 g) were depilated and then injected subcutaneously with various solutions, including PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) and CNDs (5:6) (100 μL, 160 μg/mL). Next, the injection site was treated with or without irradiation (808 nm, 0.8 W/cm2, 10 min). Mice were sacrificed on the third day of treatment and histological analysis of five major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) was performed by H&E staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antibacterial photothermal therapy of S, N co-doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: All animal procedures were approved by the Ethics Committee of Ludong University. In animal experiments, 8-week-old female BALB/c mice were divided into four groups and treated with PBS, PBS + NIR, CNDs(5:6), and CNDs(5:6)+NIR, respectively. After hind limb debridement, mice were anesthetized with isoflurane and then injected subcutaneously with 50 μL of VRE bacteria (2 × 107 CFU) and 50 μL of samples (PBS or CNDs(5:6)) in sequence, in which the final concentration of CNDs(5:6) was 160 μg/mL. After the injection site was treated with or without NIR irradiation (808 nm, 0.8 W/cm2, 10 min), we used a thermal imager to record the temperature changes at the infection site upon NIR irradiation. The abscesses at the infection site were also photographed every 24 h with a digital camera. On the third day of infection, abscess tissue was removed and analyzed by H&E staining for histological examination. Meanwhile, the infected site was cut off and put into a PBS buffer containing 1% Triton X-100 for homogenization. After gradient dilution, the homogenate was dropped on LB agar plates and incubated overnight and the number of viable bacteria was recorded for quantitative assessment of bacterial viability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Synthesis and characterizations of S, N co-doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: We have developed a one-step hydrothermal method for synthesizing S,N co-doped carbon nanodots using DMSO and FA as the single sources of S and N, respectively. Based on the volume ratio of DMSO (S) and FA (N), CNDs were classified as CNDs (11:0), CNDs (9:2), CNDs (5:6), CNDs (2:9), and CNDs (0:11). CNDs (11:0) were designated as S@CNDs, as the sulphur-containing DMSO was the sole reaction solvent. Similarly, CNDs (0:11) were designated as N@CNDs, as the nitrogen-containing FA was the sole reaction solvent. The other CNDs were named S, N co-doped CNDs, as both DMSO and FA were involved as dual reaction solvents during synthesis. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), S@CNDs had only one absorption peak in the UV region at 380 nm, while N@CNDs had two additional absorption peaks in the visible region at 480 nm and 580 nm, respectively. Notably, compared to S@CNDs and N@CNDs, S, N co-doped CNDs exhibited two absorption peaks in the visible region that were significantly red-shifted, one from 480 nm to 600 nm, and the other from 580 nm to 700 nm, resulting in significantly enhanced absorption performance in the near-infrared region (NIR). Importantly, the absorbance value of CNDs (5:6) at 800 nm was significantly higher than that of CNDs (9:2) and CNDs (2:9). Calculations based on the molecular weights of DMSO and FA revealed that the S/N molar ratio of CNDs (5:6) was close to 4:3. Thus, when S/N ratio was close to 1, the prepared S, N co-doped CNDs exhibited the greatest absorption peak red-shift and the strongest near-infrared absorption performance. Due to the strong near-infrared absorption performance of CNDs (5:6), we designated it as the representative of S, N co-doped CNDs and referred to it as SN@CNDs in subsequent experiments. Additionally, as shown in the fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 1(b), the fluorescence intensity of SN@CNDs was significantly reduced compared to S@CNDs and N@CNDs. This result suggests that co-doping S and N significantly reduces the fluorescence performance of CNDs compared to single-doping, which may be related to the change in energy dissipation mode from radiative to non-radiative [34].

We conducted a comprehensive analysis of the particle size range, charge distribution, elemental composition, and infrared spectra of S@CNDs, N@CNDs, and SN@CNDs. As depicted in Fig. 2 (a)–(b), all three CNDs demonstrated similar spherical morphologies, but exhibited different particle sizes. Specifically, the particle size of SN@CNDs (6.0 ± 2.0 nm) was larger than that of S@CNDs and N@CNDs (4.0 ± 1.0 nm and 5.0 ± 1.0 nm, respectively), suggesting that S, N co-doping is more likely to produce larger particle sizes than single doping. This outcome could be attributed to the fact that S, N co-doping results in a more uniform and smaller bandgap [33]. This finding offers a plausible explanation for the improved near-infrared absorption performance of SN@CNDs, as larger nanoparticles typically form absorption peaks in the near-infrared region.

The zeta potential distribution (Fig. 2(c)) indicates that the potential of S@CNDs (−25.2 mV) is significantly lower than that of N@CNDs (−15.1 mV). This difference can be attributed to the interaction between the carboxyl group of citric acid and the amino group of FA, resulting in a covalent reaction that reduces the number of carboxyl groups on the CNDs' surface. Interestingly, the potential of SN@CNDs is −19.6 mV, which is higher than that of S@CNDs but lower than that of N@CNDs. This finding confirms that the S/N ratio of the synthetic solvent can effectively modify the surface potential of CNDs. This phenomenon may be related to the protonation and deprotonation of sulfonic acid and amino functional groups on the nanoparticle's surface. We speculate that changes in the surface potential can induce a shift in the Fermi level, affecting the energy transition of electrons [32]. The successful co-doping of S and N in CNDs was confirmed by EDS analysis, which revealed the presence of both elements in SN@CNDs (Fig. 2(d)). Similarly, FTIR examinations demonstrated SO and N–H vibrations at 1092 cm−1 and 3100 cm−1 in SN@CNDs (Fig. 2(e)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance of S, N co-doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, we tested the photothermal performance of SN@CNDs using PBS, S@CNDs, and N@CNDs as three control groups. As shown in Fig. 3 (a),(c), the temperature of SN@CNDs increased faster and higher than that of the three control groups with the increase of NIR irradiation time, giving a temperature change of up to 50 °C. Moreover, the solution temperature also increased with the concentration of SN@CNDs and the laser intensity (Fig. 3(b) and Fig. S1(a)–(b)). From the on-off temperature change curve, it could be seen that the temperature of SN@CNDs could not change with the increase of laser irradiation (Fig. S1(c)), indicating that SN@CNDs had good photothermal stability for PTT treatment. Notably, SN@CNDs showed a photothermal conversion efficiency of up to 51.36% at 808 nm, which is much higher than that of S@CNDs and N@CNDs (Fig. 3(d)). According to previous works, we speculate that the better photothermal performance of SN@CNDs compared to CNDs and N@CNDs is due to the doping of multiple heteroatoms producing a more uniform and smaller band gap, yielding long wavelengths and narrow emission bands, which in turn leads to a shift in energy dissipation from a radioactive decay to a non-radiative decay [33,34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal therapy of S, N co-doped CNDs against VRE bacteria
Biosafety and biocompatibility of S, N co-doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The biosafety of SN@CNDs was assessed by MTT and hemolysis assays. As shown in Fig. S3(a), the viability of cells treated with SN@CNDs and darkness was up to more than 90%, while the viability of NIR-treated cells decreased with increasing concentration but remained above 70%. More importantly, no significant release of hemolysis was found in SN@CNDs at concentrations up to 160 μg/mL (Fig. S3(b)). The results of tissue H&E staining are shown in Fig. S4. No significant inflammatory factors were observed in the five major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) after treatment with SN@CNDs upon NIR irradiation or in the dark, which is consistent with the results of the PBS control groups. These data showed that SN@CNDs have the advantages of low toxicity, weak hemolysis, good biocompatibility, and maintaining the viability of eukaryotic cells under NIR irradiation, which can be used for in vivo photothermal therapy studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antibacterial photothermal therapy of S, N co-doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: A mouse subcutaneous infection model was established to assess the in vivo photothermal bactericidal performance of SN@CNDs. As shown in Fig. 6 , after NIR irradiation, the temperature of the SN@CNDs group could reach about 50 °C, which was significantly higher than the three control groups (PBS + Dark, PBS + NIR, and SN@CNDs + Dark). Moreover, the infected site of the control groups displayed significant abscesses and a large number of inflammatory cells after 3 days of treatment, while the SN@CNDs group showed a reduced abscess area and decreased inflammatory cells (Fig. S5 (a)). We quantified the in vivo photokilling effect of SN@CNDs by standard plate counting experiments of surviving bacteria in infected tissues. As shown in Fig. S5 (b), the bacteria treated with SN@CNDs and NIR irradiation decreased nearly 103-fold, which was significantly higher than the control groups, further confirming the good in vivo photokilling performance of SN@CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, we have developed a one-step hydrothermal method for the synthesis of SN@CNDs, in which DMSO and FA served as single sources of sulphur and nitrogen, respectively. By varying the volume ratio of DMSO and FA, we investigated the effect of different S/N ratios on the red-shift of the CNDs' absorption peak. Through comparative analysis of the particle size, surface charge, and fluorescence spectrum of the S@CNDs, N@CNDs, and SN@CNDs, we propose a possible mechanism to explain the change of optical properties of CNDs by S, N doping. The co-doping creates a more uniform and smaller band gap, resulting in a shift of the Fermi level, and a change in energy dissipation from radioactive to non-radiative decay. Notably, the as-prepared SN@CNDs exhibit low cytotoxicity, weak hemolysis, good biocompatibility, and high photothermal conversion efficiency, and show remarkable photokilling effects against VRE-resistant bacteria both in vitro and in vivo. Our facile method for synthesizing S and N co-doped CNDs can be extended to the preparation of other S and N co-doped nanomaterials, potentially improving their performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Xinyue Liu: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-original draft. Huaze Liu: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-original draft. Yu Wang: Formal analysis, Validation. Xueliang Zheng: Formal analysis, Validation. Hui Xu: Formal analysis, Validation. Juan Ding: Formal analysis, Validation. Jie Sun: Formal analysis, Validation. Yang Liu Formal analysis, Validation. Tingting Jiang, Qin Li, and Yang Liu: Investigation, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2020MC079, ZR2014BP004, ZR2020MB075), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505066), a project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program, China (J18KA265) and a project of Shandong Innovation and Entrepreneurship Community of Antibody Drug.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bbrc.2023.06.028.
